t: A line plot shows X marks above each number. There are 4 X marks above the number 3. How many objects measured 3 inches?
e: Each X mark means 1 object. There are 4 X marks above 3, so 4 objects measured 3 inches.

t: A line plot shows X marks above numbers. There are 2 X marks above 5 and 6 X marks above 7. Which length has more objects?
e: 5 inches has 2 X marks and 7 inches has 6 X marks. 6 is more, so 7 inches has more.

t: A line plot about pencils shows: Above 4: X X X, Above 5: X X X X X, Above 6: X X. How many pencils are 5 inches long?
e: Count the X marks above 5. There are 5 X marks, so 5 pencils are 5 inches long.

t: A line plot shows: Above 2: X, Above 3: X X X X, Above 4: X X. Which length was measured the most?
e: 3 inches has 4 X marks, which is the most. 3 inches was measured the most.

t: A line plot shows 0 X marks above the number 6. How many crayons are 6 inches long?
e: There are no X marks above 6. That means 0 crayons are 6 inches long.

t: A line plot shows X marks about worm lengths. Above 1: X X X, Above 2: X X, Above 3: X X X X X. Which length has the fewest worms?
e: 2 inches has only 2 X marks, which is the fewest.

t: A line plot shows: Above 4: X X X, Above 5: X X X, Above 6: X X X. What can you say about the data?
o: All three lengths have the same number
e: Each length has 3 X marks. They all have the same number of objects.

t: A line plot shows how long ribbons are. Above 7: X X X X X X, Above 8: X X. How many ribbons are 7 inches long?
e: Count the X marks above 7. There are 6, so 6 ribbons are 7 inches long.

t: A line plot shows: Above 3: X X, Above 4: X X X X, Above 5: X. How many sticks are 4 inches long?
e: Count the X marks above 4. There are 4, so 4 sticks are 4 inches long.

t: A line plot shows shoe lengths. Above size 5: X X X, Above size 6: X X X. Do the same number of kids wear each size?
e: Size 5 has 3 X marks and size 6 has 3 X marks. They are the same!

t: A line plot shows: Above 1: X, Above 2: X X X X, Above 3: X X, Above 4: X X X. Which length has the most?
e: 2 inches has 4 X marks, which is the most.

t: A line plot shows leaf lengths. Above 5: X X X X, Above 6: no X marks, Above 7: X X. Were any leaves 6 inches long?
e: There are 0 X marks above 6. No leaves were 6 inches long.

t: A line plot shows hand widths. Above 3: X X X X X, Above 4: X X X, Above 5: X X X X. Put the lengths in order from fewest to most X marks.
e: 3 inches = 5, 4 inches = 3, 5 inches = 4. From fewest to most: 4 (3 marks), 5 (4 marks), 3 (5 marks).

t: A line plot shows: Above 8: X X X X, Above 9: X, Above 10: X X. How many objects measured 9 inches?
e: There is 1 X mark above 9. So 1 object measured 9 inches.

t: A line plot about bean lengths shows: Above 2: X X, Above 3: X X X X X X, Above 4: X X X. Which length was measured the most?
e: 3 inches has 6 X marks, which is the most. 3 inches was measured the most.

t: A line plot shows: Above 6: X X X, Above 7: X X X X X, Above 8: X X. How many strings are 8 inches long?
e: Count the X marks above 8. There are 2, so 2 strings are 8 inches long.

t: A line plot shows caterpillar lengths. Above 1: X X, Above 2: X X X, Above 3: X X X X. Which length has the fewest caterpillars?
e: 1 inch has only 2 X marks, which is the fewest.

t: A line plot shows: Above 4: X X X X, Above 5: X X X X. How many items are 4 or 5 inches long in total?
e: 4 inches has 4 marks and 5 inches has 4 marks. 4 + 4 = 8 items total.

t: A line plot shows eraser widths. Above 1: X X X, Above 2: X X X X X, Above 3: X X. Which width was most common?
e: 2 inches has 5 X marks, which is the most. 2 inches was the most common width.

t: A line plot shows: Above 5: X X, Above 6: X X X X, Above 7: X X X, Above 8: X. What is the smallest length shown with X marks?
e: The smallest number with X marks above it is 5. So 5 inches is the smallest length.
